verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And it came to pass, that after three days Paul called the chief of the Jews together: and when they were come together, he said unto them, Men and brethren, though I have committed nothing against the people, or customs of our fathers, yet was I delivered prisoner from Jerusalem into the hands of the Romans.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who, when they had examined me, would have let me go, because there was no cause of death in me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when the Jews spake against it, I was constrained to appeal unto Cæsar; not that I had aught to accuse my nation of.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this cause therefore have I called for you, to see you, and to speak with you: because that for the hope of Israel I am bound with this chain.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He wished to exculpate both himself and others; himself, that they might not accuse him, and by so doing hurt themselves; and those (others), that it might not seem that the whole thing was of their doing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it was likely that a report was prevalent, that he had been delivered up by the Jews; and this was enough to alarm them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He therefore addresses himself to this, and defends himself as to his own conduct.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “How then is it reasonable,” it might be said, “that they should deliver thee up without a cause?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Roman governors, he says, bear me witness, who wished to let me go.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “How was it then that they did not let (thee) go?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “When the Jews spake against it,” he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he extenuates (in speaking of) their charges against him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since if he had wished to aggravate matters, he might have used them so as to bear harder upon them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore, he says, “I was constrained to appeal unto Cæsar:” so that his whole speech is of a forgiving nature.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then? didst thou this, that thou mightest accuse them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No, he says: “Not that I had aught to accuse my nation of:” but that I might escape the danger.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is for your sakes “that I am bound with this chain.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So far am I, he says, from any hostile feeling towards you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then they also were so subdued by his speech, that they too apologized for those of their own nation: “And they said unto him, We neither received letters out of Judea concerning thee, neither any of the brethren that came showed or spake any harm of thee.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Neither through letters, nor through men, have they made known any harm of thee.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nevertheless, we wish to hear from thyself: “But we desire to hear of thee what thou thinkest” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22): and then forestalled him by showing their own sentiments.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For as concerning this sect, it is known to us, that everywhere it is spoken against.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when they had appointed him a day, there came many to him into his lodging; to whom he expounded and testified the kingdom of God, persuading them concerning Jesus, both out of the Law of Moses and out of the Prophets, from morning till evening.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And some believed the things which were spoken, and some believed not.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They said not, we speak against it, but “it is spoken against.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he did not immediately answer, but gave them a day, and they came to him, and he discoursed, it says, “both out of the Law of Moses, and out of the Prophets.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And some believed, and some believed not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when they agreed not among themselves, they departed, after that Paul had spoken one word, Well spake the Holy Ghost by Esaias the prophet unto our fathers, saying, Go unto this people, and say, Hearing ye shall hear, and shall not understand; and seeing ye shall see, and not perceive: for the heart of this people is waxed gross, and their ears are dull of hearing, and their eyes have they closed; lest they should see with their eyes, and hear with their ears, and understand with their heart, and should be converted, and I should heal them.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when they departed, as they were opposed to each other, then he reproaches them, not because he wished to reproach those (that believed not), but to confirm these (that believed).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Well said Esaias,” says he to them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that to the Gentiles it is given to know this mystery.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No wonder then, if they did gainsay: this was foretold from the first.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then again he moves their jealousy (on the score) of them of the Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Be it known therefore unto you, that the salvation of God is sent unto the Gentiles, and that they will hear it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when he had said these words, the Jews departed, and had great reasoning among themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Paul dwelt two whole years in his own hired house, and received all that came in unto him, preaching the kingdom of God, and teaching those things which concern the Lord Jesus Christ, with all confidence, no man forbidding him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It shows the freedom he had now: without hindrance in Rome, he who had been hindered in Judea; and he remained teaching there for two years.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What of the (years) after these?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.) (d) “Who having examined me,” says he, “found nothing in me” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When those ought to have rescued, they “delivered (him) into the hands of the Romans.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And such the superabundance, * * because those had not power to condemn but delivered him prisoner.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Not as having aught to accuse my nation of,” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 19) am I come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See what friendliness of expression “my nation:” he does not hold them as aliens.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not say, I do not accuse, but, “I have not (whereof) to accuse:” although he had suffered so many evils at their hands.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But nothing of all this does he say, nor make his speech offensive: neither does he seem to be sparing them as matter of favor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this was the main point, to show that they delivered him prisoner to the Romans, when those ought to have condemned him. (a) “For this cause,” he says, “I wished to see you” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 20): that it might not be in any man’s power to accuse me, and to say what (naturally) might suggest itself (τὰ παριστάμενα), that having escaped their hands I have come for this: not to bring evils upon others, but myself fleeing from evils.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I was compelled to appeal unto Cæsar.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe them also speaking more mildly to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “We beg,” say they: and wish to speak in exculpation of those (at Jerusalem). (e) Whereas they ought to accuse them, they plead for them: by the very fact of their exonerating them, they do in fact accuse them. (b) For this very thing was a proof that they knew themselves exceedingly in the wrong.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Had they been confident, they would at any rate have done this, so that he should not have it in his power to make out his story in his own way, and besides they shrank from coming.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And by their many times attempting they showed * * (f) “As for this sect, it is known to us,” say they, “that it is everywhere spoken against.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, but (people) are also everywhere persuaded (as, in fact, here), “some were persuaded, and some believed not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when they had appointed him a day,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See again how not by miracles but by Law and Prophets he puts them to silence, and how we always find him doing this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet he might also have wrought signs; but then it would no longer have been matter of faith.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In fact, this (itself) was a great sign, his discoursing from the Law and the Prophets.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then that you may not deem it strange (that they believed not), he introduces the prophecy which saith, “Hearing ye shall hear and not understand,” more now than then: “and ye shall see and not perceive” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 26) more now than then.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is not spoken for the former sort, but for the unbelievers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Was it contrary to the prophecy, that those believed? (“Go,”) it says, “unto this people” (that is), to the unbelieving people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He did not say this to insult them, but to remove the offence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Be it known then,” he says, “unto you, that unto the Gentiles is sent the salvation of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They,” says he, “will hear it too.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then why dost thou discourse to us?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Didst thou not know this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes, but that ye might be persuaded, and that I might exculpate myself, and give none a handle (against me). (c) The unbelieving were they that withdrew.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But see how they do not now form plots against him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in Judea they had a sort of tyranny.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then wherefore did the Providence of God order that he should go thither, and yet the Lord had said, “Get thee out quickly from Jerusalem?” (ch. xxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That both their wickedness might be shown and Christ’s prophecy made good, that they would not endure to hear him: and so that all might learn that he was ready to suffer all things, and that the event might be for the consolation of those in Judea: for there also (the brethren) were suffering many grievous evils.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if while preaching the Jewish doctrines, he suffered thus, had he preached the doctrines of the glory of Christ, how would they have endured him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: While “purifying himself” (ch. xxi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 26) he was intolerable, and how should he have been tolerable while preaching?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What lay ye to his charge?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What have ye heard?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He spoke nothing of the kind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was simply seen, and he exasperated all against him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well might he then be set apart for the Gentiles: well might he be sent afar off: there also destined to discourse to the Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: First he calls the Jews, then having shown them the facts he comes to the Gentiles. (ch. xxiv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Well spake the Holy Ghost,” etc. But this saying, “The Spirit said,” is nothing wonderful: for an angel also is said to say what the Lord saith: but He (the Spirit) not so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When one is speaking of the things said by the angel, one does not say, Well said the angel, but, Well said the Lord.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Well said the Spirit:” as much as to say, It is not me that ye disbelieve.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But God foreknew this from the first.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He discoursed,” it says, “with boldness, unhindered” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 31): for it is possible to speak with boldness, yet hindered.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: His boldness nothing checked: but in fact he also spoke unhindered. (c) “Discoursed,” it says, “the things concerning the kingdom of God:” mark, nothing of the things of sense, nothing of the things present. (f) But of his affairs after the two years, what say we? (b) (The writer) leaves the hearer athirst for more: the heathen authors do the same (in their writings), for to know everything makes the reader dull and jaded.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or else he does this, (e) not having it in his power to exhibit it from his own personal knowledge. (a) Mark the order of God’s Providence, “I have been much hindered from coming unto you…having a great desire these many years to come unto you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22, 23.) (d) But he fed them with hopes. (g) I am in haste to go to Spain, and “I hope,” says he, “to see you in my journey, and to be brought thitherward on my journey by you, if first I be filled with your company in some measure.” (ib.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 24.) (i) Of this he says, I will come and rest together with you “in the fulness of the blessing of the Gospel” (ib.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 29): and again “I am going to Jerusalem to minister to the saints” (ib.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 25): this is the same that he has said here, “To do alms to my nation I came.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17.) (h) Do you mark how he did not foresee everything—that sacred and divine head, the man higher than the heavens, that had a soul able to grasp all at once, the holder of the first place—Paul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The man whose very name, to them that know him, suffices for rousing of the soul, for vigilance, for shaking off all sleep!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Rome received him bound, coming up from the sea, saved from a ship-wreck—and was saved from the shipwreck of error.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Like an emperor that has fought a naval battle and overcome, he entered into that most imperial city. (k) He was nearer now to his crown.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Rome received him bound, and saw him crowned and proclaimed conqueror.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There he had said, I will rest together with you: but this was the beginning of a course once more, and he added trophies to trophies, a man not to be overcome.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Corinth kept him two years, and Asia three, and this city two for this time; a second time he again entered it, when also he was consummated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus he escaped then, and having filled the whole world, he so brought his life to a close.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why didst thou wish to learn what happened after these two years?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Those too are such as these: bonds, tortures, fightings, imprisonments, lyings in wait, false accusations, deaths, day by day.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou hast seen but a small part of it?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How much soever thou hast seen, such is he for all the rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As in the case of the sky, if thou see one part of it, go where thou wilt thou shalt see it such as this: as it is with the sun, though thou see its rays but in part, thou mayest conjecture the rest: so is it with Paul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: His Acts thou hast seen in part; such are they all throughout, teeming with dangers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was a heaven having in it the Sun of Righteousness, not such a sun (as we see): so that that man was better than the very heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Think you that this is a small thing—when you say “The Apostle,” immediately every one thinks of him (as), when you say “The Baptist,” immediately they think of John?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To what shall one compare his words?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To the sea, or even to the ocean?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But nothing is equal to them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: More copious than this (sea) are (his) streams; purer and deeper; so that one would not err in calling Paul’s heart both a sea and a heaven, the one for purity, the other for depth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is a sea, having for its voyagers not those who sail from city to city, but those from earth to heaven: if any man sail in this sea, he will have a prosperous voyage.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On this sea, not winds, but instead of winds the Holy and Divine Spirit wafts the souls which sail thereon: no waves are here, no rock, no monsters: all is calm.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a sea which is more calm and secure than a haven, having no bitter brine, but a pure fountain both sweeter than * *, and brighter and more transparent than the sun: a sea it is, not having precious stones, nor purple dye as ours, but treasures far better than those.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He who wishes to descend into this sea, needs not divers, needs not oil, but much loving-kindness (φιλανθρωπίας): he will find in it all the good things that are in the kingdom of Heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He will even be able to become a king, and to take the whole world into his possession, and to be in the greatest honor; he who sails on this sea will never undergo shipwreck, but will know all things well.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But as those who are inexpert in this (our visible sea) are suffocated (in attempting to dive therein), so is it in that other sea: which is just the case with the heretics, when they attempt things above their strength.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It behooves therefore to know the depth, or else not to venture.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If we are to sail on this sea, let us come well-girded.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I could not,” he says, “speak unto you as unto spiritual, but as unto carnal.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (1 Cor. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let no one who is without endurance sail on this sea.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us provide for ourselves ships, that is, zeal, earnestness, prayers, that we may pass over the sea in quiet.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For indeed this is the living water.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Like as if one should get a mouth of fire, such a mouth does that man get who knows Paul well: like as if one should have a sharp sword, so again does such an one become invincible.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And for the understanding of Paul’s words there is needed also a pure life.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For therefore also he said: “Ye are become such as have need of milk, seeing ye are dull of hearing.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there is, there is an infirmity of hearing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as a stomach which is infirm could not take in wholesome food (which it finds) hard of digestion, so a soul which is become tumid and heated, unstrung and relaxed, could not receive the word of the Spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hear the disciples saying, “This is a hard saying: who can hear it” (John vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if the soul be strong and healthy, all is most easy, all is light: it becomes more lofty and buoyant: it is more able to soar and lift itself on high.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Knowing then these things, let us bring our soul into a healthy state: let us emulate Paul, and imitate that noble, that adamantine soul: that, advancing in the steps of his life, we may be enabled to sail through the sea of this present life, and to come unto the haven wherein are no waves, and attain unto the good things promised to them that love Him, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father and Holy Ghost together be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
